text: Niŋi n na, daba maa yeli u, “Za zinaa wara, e yuuri bi lee n niŋi Yakubu, e yuuri n niŋi Izireel, a kpali bo? E ni Naawunni ni niriba n mo, e nyaŋŋi ba la zu.”

ref: Jacob said, “Now tell me your name.” “Don't you know who I am?” he asked. And he blessed Jacob.
text: Yakubu yeli u, “Wusi ma e yuuri.” “Bo zu n na e kpabira e baŋŋi m yuuri?” Ka nafa Yakubu.

ref: The sun was coming up as Jacob was leaving Peniel. He was limping because he had been struck on the hip,
text: Wuluŋu daa n pusini poin, ka Yakubu yi Peniyel, ka kpagsira o kpiiligu la niŋi.

ref: and the muscle on his hip joint had been injured. That's why even today the people of Israel don't eat the hip muscle of any animal.
text: Halli a ti yinni zinni, n ŋa zu n na Izireel dima bi dira buŋkoo gberi tomu ziya, a kpali Naawunni ni daa n salli Yakubu kpiiligu la zu.

ref: As Jacob was on his way back home, some of God's angels came and met him.
text: Niŋi n na, Yakubu daa lee n naa soori wara, Naawunni malaakasi ti paag u.

ref: When Jacob saw them, he said, “This is God's camp.” So he named the place Mahanaim.
text: O n si n nye ba, o yeli, “Naawunni ziiziya n ŋa.” Ka digili niŋi yuuri Mahanayim.

ref: Jacob sent messengers on ahead to Esau, who lived in the land of Seir, also known as Edom.
text: Niŋi n na, Yakubu tumi o tumusi, ba pa yii we o beeri Isawu saani, a yi Seya n ba Idom paaligu.

ref: Jacob told them to say to Esau, “Master, I am your servant! I have lived with Laban all this time,
text: Yatogso o n naa ti tumusi maa ba wa ti ti o beeri la n niŋi, ni ba yeli u, ni o nachiima, Yakubu, so n daa n ba Leban saani yuu, ka bi chena yiri la n na.

ref: and now I own cattle, donkeys, and sheep, as well as many slaves. Master, I am sending these messengers in the hope that you will be kind to me.”

ref: So tell him, ‘They belong to your servant Jacob, who is coming this way. He is sending them as a gift to his master Esau.’ ”
text: e yeli u, ‘A niŋini e nachiima Yakubu bini, o tumi ti, ni ti naa n ti ti i, ama o gba ba nyaaŋa chena.’”

ref: Jacob also told the men in charge of the second and third herds and those who followed to say the same thing when they met Esau.

text: A zu, o ti ni piini ŋa pa yiya, ka ba do nisaaniŋi. Seema Yakubu ni Naawunni n daa n mo n ŋa

ref: Jacob got up in the middle of the night and took his wives, his eleven children, and everything he owned across to the other side of the Jabbok River for safety.
text: O n mari ba du mogiri maa baasi, o pori n ti mari o bunso zaa n che du.

ref: Afterwards, Jacob went back and spent the rest of the night alone. A man came and fought with Jacob until just before daybreak.

ref: When the man saw that he could not win, he struck Jacob on the hip and threw it out of joint.
text: Lala daba maa n nyaasi, ka o ki tee n nyaŋŋi Yakubu, o salli Yakubu kpiiligu, o kpiiligu maa furisi.

ref: Then the man asked, “What is your name?” “Jacob,” he answered.
text: Daba maa boosi u. “E yuuri?” “M yuuri n niŋi Yakubu.”

ref: The man said, “From now on, your name will no longer be Jacob. You will be called Israel, because you have wrestled with God and with men, and you have won.”
text: Niŋi n na, daba maa yeli u, “Za zinaa wara, e yuuri bi lee n niŋi Yakubu, e yuuri n niŋi Izireel, a kpali bo? E ni Naawunni ni niriba n mo, e nyaŋŋi ba la zu.”

ref: Jacob said, “Now tell me your name.” “Don't you know who I am?” he asked. And he blessed Jacob.
text: Yakubu yeli u, “Wusi ma e yuuri.” “Bo zu n na e kpabira e baŋŋi m yuuri?” Ka nafa Yakubu.

ref: The sun was coming up as Jacob was leaving Peniel. He was limping because he had been struck on the hip,
text: Wuluŋu daa n pusini poin, ka Yakubu yi Peniyel, ka kpagsira o kpiiligu la niŋi.

ref: and the muscle on his hip joint had been injured. That's why even today the people of Israel don't eat the hip muscle of any animal.
text: Halli a ti yinni zinni, n ŋa zu n na Izireel dima bi dira buŋkoo gberi tomu ziya, a kpali Naawunni ni daa n salli Yakubu kpiiligu la zu.

ref: As Jacob was on his way back home, some of God's angels came and met him.
text: Niŋi n na, Yakubu daa lee n naa soori wara, Naawunni malaakasi ti paag u.

ref: When Jacob saw them, he said, “This is God's camp.” So he named the place Mahanaim.
text: O n si n nye ba, o yeli, “Naawunni ziiziya n ŋa.” Ka digili niŋi yuuri Mahanayim.

ref: Jacob sent messengers on ahead to Esau, who lived in the land of Seir, also known as Edom.
text: Niŋi n na, Yakubu tumi o tumusi, ba pa yii we o beeri Isawu saani, a yi Seya n ba Idom paaligu.

ref: Jacob told them to say to Esau, “Master, I am your servant! I have lived with Laban all this time,
text: Yatogso o n naa ti tumusi maa ba wa ti ti o beeri la n niŋi, ni ba yeli u, ni o nachiima, Yakubu, so n daa n ba Leban saani yuu, ka bi chena yiri la n na.

ref: and now I own cattle, donkeys, and sheep, as well as many slaves. Master, I am sending these messengers in the hope that you will be kind to me.”
text: Ni o marini naagi, buŋsi, peeri, buusi, a ni gurunsi dabsi ni pogiba, ka o suusira i maa e zo u nimbaa.

ref: When the messengers returned, they told Jacob, “We went to your brother Esau, and now he is heading this way with
text: Tumusi maa n pori n ta, n ŋa n na ba yeli Yakubu, “Ti wa ti yeli e beeri Isawu, o mari niriba kobsinaasi tuusira i.”

ref: Jacob was so frightened that he divided his people, sheep, cattle, and camels into two groups.
text: Niŋi n na, dabeem ni sufusaaŋu gbaa Yakubu, a zu, o barigi o niriba la ni buusi, peeri, naagi, a ni nyogimasi tulum buyaayi zu.

ref: He thought, “If Esau attacks one group, perhaps the other can escape.”
text: A kpali bo, o feni maa, ka Isawu ti zabi n nyaŋŋi tulum buluŋko, seesi n che la ni sogi n zo.

ref: Then Jacob prayed: You, Lord, are the God who was worshiped by my grandfather Abraham and by my father Isaac. You told me to return home to my family, and you promised to be with me and make me successful.
text: Niŋi n na, Yakubu suusi Naawunni, “M yaadaba Ibrahim ni m ba Yisaaka Naawunni, m yidaana, nyini n daa n yeli ma, ni m pori n gaa m paaligu, m niriba saani, e ni ti bunso zaa chaŋŋi nene ti ma.

ref: Please rescue me from my brother. I am afraid he will come and attack not only me, but my wives and children as well.
text: A zu, m suusira i ni e moligi ma yi m beeri Isawu nuusi niŋi. M zorini dabeem maa o chena o ti zabi m ni pogiba ni biisi toowa.

ref: But you have promised that I would be a success and that someday it will be as hard to count my descendants as it is to count the grains of sand along the seashore.
text: Ama, nyini n daa n yeli ma, ni e n ti m loobi bindaana, ka ti m baligu saligi maa teeku puu tambisigu seesi di n ki tee n kaali.”

ref: After Jacob had spent the night there, he chose some animals as gifts for Esau:
text: Yakubu daa doni niŋi kaa bee, ka nyisi binseesi o naa wa ti pi o beeri Isawu piini.

ref: And Jacob told them to be sure to say that he was right behind them. Jacob hoped the gifts would make Esau friendly, so Esau would be glad to see him when they met.
text: Ya daŋŋi n yeli u, “E nachiima Yakubu ba nyaaŋa chena.” Yakubu daa feni maa, “Ka o naa n ŋa piini pa yii wa ti ti o beeri Isawu, o puu ni peligi, ka o deeg u nuusi ayi.”

ref: Jacob said, “I have seen God face to face, and I am still alive.” So he named the place Peniel.
text: Niŋi n na, Yakubu yeli, “M nye Naawunni nufu niŋi, ka ma na mari nyevuri.” A zu, Yakubu digili niŋi yuuri Peniyel.

ref: The two servant women, Zilpah and Bilhah, together with their children went first, followed by Leah and her children, then by Rachel and Joseph.
text: O ti bipogiba bayi la ni ba biisi pa yiya, Liya ni o biisi pa, ka Rachel ni Yisifu pa nyaaŋa.

ref: Jacob himself walked in front of them all, bowing to the ground seven times as he came near his brother.
text: Ba n ti paagira o beeri, Yakubu gaa yii wa ti gba u tiŋŋa buyapoin.

ref: But Esau ran toward Jacob and hugged and kissed him. Then the two brothers started crying.
text: Isawu chee n ti ŋaami u tabi o puwa, a naag o kpuŋkpama vili o nuŋgori, ka mugisi u, ka ba zaa kumina.

ref: When Esau noticed the women and children he asked, “Whose children are these?” Jacob answered, “These are the children the Lord has been kind enough to give to me, your servant.”
text: Niŋi n na, Isawu nye pogiba ni biisi la, ka boosi u, “Ya saani dima n ŋa?” Yakubu yeli u, “Biseesi Naawunni n zo e nachiima nimbaa, ka ti u n na la.”

ref: Then the two servant women and their children came and bowed down to Esau.
text: Bipogiba la ni ba biisi ti gba tiŋŋa.

ref: Next, Leah and her children came and bowed down; finally, Joseph and Rachel also came and bowed down.
text: Seesi n lee n pa, Liya ni o biisi ti gba tiŋŋa. A baasigu, Yisifu ni Rachel gba ti gba tiŋŋa.

ref: Esau asked Jacob, “Why did you send those herds I met along the road?” “Master,” Jacob answered, “I sent them so you would be friendly to me.”
text: Isawu boosi u, “Ka buŋkobseesi ni tuusi la mini, bo n na a mi wusi?” Yakubu yeli u, “M yidaana, e piini n na, m nye seema ti mabiilim di n gbaa taaba.”

ref: “But, brother, I already have plenty,” Esau replied. “Keep them for yourself.”
text: Isawu yeli u, “M sunzo, m sali n mari buŋgbaliŋu, mari e bini.”

ref: “No!” Jacob said. “Please accept them as a sign of your friendship for me. When you welcomed me and I saw your face, it was like seeing the face of God.
text: Yakubu suusi u, “Jaande, ka a yi peligi e sufu maa ni za e saana, dee piini so ni ma n ti ti i ŋa. Seema ni si n za e saani a, a niŋini maa ni za Naawunni saani.

ref: Please accept these as gifts I brought to you. God has been good to me, and I have everything I need.” Jacob kept insisting until Esau agreed.
text: Jaande, dee binseesi ni ma n ti ti i ŋa. Naawunni zo ma ni nimbaa, ka ti ma bunso zaa ni kpabira.” A kpali Yakubu n suusi Isawu zu, o deeg a.

ref: “Let's get ready to travel,” Esau said. “I'll go along with you.”
text: Isawu yeli u, “Niŋi siriya ti gaari, m ni pa i yiya.”

ref: But Jacob answered, “Master, you know traveling is hard on children, and I have to look after the sheep and goats that are nursing their young. If my animals travel too much in one day, they will all die.
text: Ama Yakubu yeli u, “M yidaana, e baŋiya maa biisaa na ka kpeeŋu, ka a daga maa m dasi penyaana ni naginyaana seesi n na mari bibaala la niŋi. Ka ti si n kari a kpeeŋtiri dindaluŋko lala, buŋkobiri maa zaa ni kpi.

ref: Why don't you go on ahead and let me travel along slowly with the children, the herds, and the flocks. We can meet again in the country of Edom.”
text: A zu, m yidaana, paara e nachiima yiya, ka m mari buŋkobiri la ni biisi maa chaŋina boinboin, a ti paag i yi Idom paaligu.”

ref: Esau replied, “Let me leave some of my men with you.” “You don't have to do that,” Jacob answered. “I am happy, simply knowing that you are friendly to me.”
text: Isawu yeli u, “M ni ti e ni m niribaa seesi ya chena.” Yakubu yeli u, “Da niŋi lala. O bi tiri n ti, m si n kpabira ni m nye seema e sufu n peligi m niŋi.”

ref: But Jacob went to Succoth, where he built a house for himself and set up shelters for his animals. That's why the place is called Succoth. Jacob Arrives at Shechem
text: Yakubu gaa Sukoti me dii, ka maali o buŋkobiri beebu ziisi. A zu n na ba boona nisaaniŋi Sukoti la.

ref: After leaving northern Syria, Jacob arrived safely at Shechem in Canaan and set up camp outside the city.
text: Yakubu n yi Mesopoteemiya paaligu la, o paa Sekem n niŋi Keenan dima tiŋŋa, a ni alaanfiya, ka sabi o gbana suga, a zi nisaaniŋi, a leeli tiŋŋa maa.

ref: The land where he camped was owned by the descendants of Hamor, the father of Shechem. So Jacob paid them
text: Yakubu dani tiŋgbaŋŋu maa yi Hamo, so n niŋi Sekem ba, biisi saani. Gbitisi koowa n na o da u.

ref: then he set up his tents and built an altar there to honor the God of Israel.
text: Nisaaniŋi n na Yakubu me Naawunni sariga daaŋu, ka digili u El-Elohe Izireel.

ref: Dinah, the daughter of Jacob and Leah, went to visit some of the women who lived nearby.
text: Dindaso n na, Dayina, so n niŋi Yakubu ni Liya bipuŋŋu la, yi n gaa o taaba pogiba saani yi tiŋŋa maa puwa.

ref: She was seen by Hamor's son Shechem, the leader of the Hivites, and he grabbed her and raped her.
text: Niŋi n na, Sekem, Hamo so n niŋi Hivati nira, ka niŋi paaligu maa naaba biduwa, nye Dayina, ka gbaag u wa ti bibi u kpabi.

ref: But Shechem was attracted to Dinah, so he told her how much he loved her.
text: Ama, o n nye bipuŋŋu maa, o yela peri u ga, o togsi u kpabirisim yela, a kpabira maa o loobi o poga.

ref: Shechem even asked his father to arrange for him to marry her.
text: Sekem yeli o ba Hamo, “Naa bipuŋŋaa ti ma, o niŋi m poga.”

ref: Meanwhile, Jacob heard what had happened. But his sons were out in the fields with the cattle, so he did not do anything at the time.
text: Yakubu n wumi maa ba saami o bii Dayina la, ka o biduusi na mari o buŋkobiri ba moopuwa, o na muuri yelli maa, a ti paa jemanso ba n ta yiri.

ref: just as Jacob's sons were coming in from work. When they learned that their sister had been raped, they became furiously angry, because nothing is more disgraceful than rape, and it must not be tolerated.
text: Yakubu biduusi n yi moo puu ta, ba wumi yaso n niŋi maa, sufusaaŋu ni suuri gbaa ba ga, seema Sekem n niŋi n ŋa ŋaaba yelli, a yi Yakubu biisi puwa, a kpabi Yakubu biya, yaso n di n bi daga o niŋi.

ref: Hamor said to Jacob and his sons: My son Shechem really loves Dinah. Please let him marry her.
text: Hamo yeli Yakubu, “M bii Sekem kpabira e bipuŋŋaa ga. Jaande, naag u ti u, o niŋi o poga.

ref: Why don't you start letting your families marry into our families and ours marry into yours?
text: Ti naagira taaba, ya ti ti naagira ya bipunsi, ka ya gba ya naagira ti bipunsi.

ref: You can share this land with us. Move freely about until you find the property you want; then buy it and settle down here.
text: Ya ni tee n zi tiŋgbaŋaa niŋi, ya niŋi bunso zaa ya n kpabira. Ya zi o niŋi, a dira yawu, a nye bindira yi o niŋi”.

ref: Shechem added, “Do this favor for me, and I'll give whatever you want.
text: Niŋi n na, Sekem yeli Dayina ba ni o sunzodabisi, “Ya paasi m niŋi, ka bunso zaa ya n kpabira, m ni ti ya.

ref: Jacob's sons wanted to get even with Shechem and his father because of what had happened to their sister.
text: A kpali ba n sagimi ba sunzopoga Dayina zu, Yakubu biduusi yomsi Sekem ni o ba Hamo.

ref: So they tricked them by saying: You're not circumcised! It would be a disgrace for us to let you marry Dinah now.
text: Ba yeli ba, “Ti ki tee n niŋi lala, ti ki tee n naa ti sunzopoga ti daba so yoori n bi ŋma, o lala n niŋi ŋaaba ti ti.

ref: But we will let you marry her, if you and the other men in your tribe agree to be circumcised.
text: Ti ni zaali ya zaaligu luŋko, a maa, ya ba seema ti ni be ŋa, dabsi zaa ba ŋma ba yoya.

ref: Then your families can marry into ours, and ours can marry into yours, and we can live together like one nation.
text: Lala wune, ti ni naa ti bipunsi tira ya, ka ti gba ti naagira ya bipunsi. Ti zi ya puwa, ti ni ya zaa ti loobi luŋko.

ref: But if you don't agree to be circumcised, we'll take Dinah and leave this place.
text: Ama, ka ya yi bi sagi maa, ya ni ŋma ya yoya wune, ti ni mari ti sunzopoga gaari.”

ref: Hamor and Shechem liked what was said.
text: Bunso ba n yeli maa ŋmaani yasuŋŋu ti Hamo ni o bii Sekem.

ref: Shechem was the most respected person in his family, and he was so in love with Dinah that he hurried off to get everything done.
text: Bipollaa, so n niŋi jilima zaa daana, a daga, a yi o ba laanta, o bi lee n mari yela maa seema ba n yeli maa yuu, a kpali, o daa n kpabira Yakubu bipuŋŋu maa ga.

ref: The two men met with the other leaders of their city and told them:
text: A zu, Hamo ni o bii Sekem yi n gaa tiŋŋa maa voori noori, seela ba n laŋsira, ba wa ti togsi n ti tiŋŋa maa dima.

ref: These people really are friendly. Why not let them move freely about until they find the property they want? There's enough land here for them and for us. Then our families can marry into theirs, and theirs can marry into ours.
text: Ba yeli ba, “Niribaa niŋini nivumansi ti ti, ya cheli ti ti ba zi tiŋgbaŋaa niŋi, ba dira yawu, zii maa yeligiya ba n di n zi, ti ni tee n naagira ba bipunsi, ka ba gba ba naagira ti bipunsi.

ref: We have to do only one thing before they will agree to stay here and become one nation with us. Our men will have to be circumcised just like theirs.
text: Niriba maa ni sagi ti ni ba ti zi maa nivu luŋko, ka ti sagiya maa ti ni ŋma ti yoya, a maa barima.

ref: Just think! We'll get their property, as well as their flocks and herds. All we have to do is to agree, and they will live here with us.
text: Ba buŋguuna ŋa ni ba bindaalim binaa zaa ki loobi ti bini? A zu, ya ti ti wumi n ti ba, ka ti ni ba ti zi”.

ref: Every grown man followed this advice and got circumcised. Dinah's Brothers Take Revenge
text: Niseesi zaa ni laŋsi maa wumi n ti Hamo ni o bii Sekem, ka daba zaa n ba tiŋŋa maa puu ŋma ba yoya.

ref: Three days later the men who had been circumcised were still weak from pain. So Simeon and Levi, two of Dinah's brothers, attacked with their swords and killed every man in the town,
text: Dabaa ata nyaaŋa, jemanso niriba maa zaa ni ba beerim niŋi, Yakubu biduusi bayi, Simiyon ni Livai, Dayina sunzodabisi naa ba tokobisi kpe tiŋŋa maa puwa, seema niriba maa ni bi fe, a ku ba dabsi zaa nyem.

ref: including Hamor and Shechem. Then they took Dinah and left.
text: Ba ku Hamo ni o bii Sekem, ka kpe Sekem dii mari Dayina yi n gaa.

ref: Jacob's other sons came and took everything they wanted. All this was done because of the horrible thing that had happened to their sister.
text: Niriba maa kuubu nyaaŋa, Yakubu biduseesi n che la kpe tiŋŋa maa puu nye kpiinsi maa, ka kaasi ba kapete bini, a kpali ba n sagimi ba sunzopoga la zu.

ref: They took sheep, goats, donkeys, and everything else that was in the town or the countryside.
text: Ba gbaa ba peeri, buusi, naagi, a ni buŋsi ni bunso zaa n ba tiŋŋa maa puwa, a ni ba puuri niŋi.

ref: After taking everything of value from the houses, they dragged away the wives and children of their victims.
text: Ba naa ba bindaalim bini zaa, a ni ba pogiba ni biisi, a kaasi ba diisi puu bini zaa, a di ba afuwa.

ref: Jacob said to Simeon and Levi, “Look what you've done! Now I'm in real trouble with the Canaanites and Perizzites who live around here. There aren't many of us, and if they attack, they'll kill everyone in my household.”
text: Niŋi n na, Yakubu yeli Simiyon ni Livai, “Ya kpabi yela ti ma, ya ti ma yubee yi niseesi n ba tiŋgbaŋaa niŋi, Keenan dima ni Periziti dima balisaa. Ti bi zoo, ba wune si n laŋsi taaba, ba ni ku ma, m ni m laanta zaa ni muri.”

ref: They answered, “Was it right to let our own sister be treated that way?”
text: Ba yeli u, “Seema o n niŋi ti sunzopogaa, a maa jagimilo ŋa, o veela?”

ref: God told Jacob, “Return to Bethel, where I appeared to you when you were running from your brother Esau. Make your home there and build an altar for me.”
text: Niŋi n na, Naawunni yeli Yakubu “Nyisigi n gaa Betel wa ti zi, ka me sariga daaŋu ti mani Naawunni, so n daa n ta e saani, ka e daa n kpali e mabii Isawu zu zora la”.

ref: Jacob said to his family and to everyone else who was traveling with him: Get rid of your foreign gods! Then make yourselves acceptable to worship God and put on clean clothes.
text: Yakubu yeli o laanta dima zaa, “Ya naa ya buga zaa basi, ka niŋi ya maŋsi nyem, a cherigi ya bimpinna.

ref: Afterwards, we'll go to Bethel. I will build an altar there for God, who answered my prayers when I was in trouble and who has always been at my side.
text: Ka ya ta ti gaa Betel. Nisaaniŋi n na, ma ya n me sariga daaŋu ti Naawunni, so n daa n wumi m suusigu, jemanso ni daa n ba sufusaaŋu niŋi, ka ba m saani seela zaa ni wara”.

ref: So everyone gave Jacob their idols and their earrings, and he buried them under the oak tree near Shechem.
text: A zu, ba naa ba buga ni ba kundesi zaa ti Yakubu, o pi a, a yi tikarigu gbinni, a leeli Sekem.

ref: While Jacob and his family were traveling through Canaan, God terrified the people in the towns so much that no one dared bother them.
text: Niŋi n na, ba yi soori, Naawunni ti dabeem gbaa tiŋseesi n poli n jili ba maa zaa, lala zu, nira zaa bi kari n pa ba zu.

ref: Finally, they reached Bethel, also known as Luz.
text: Yakubu ni o niriba maa zaa ta Luzi , a yi Keenan dima tiŋgbaŋŋu niŋi.

text: Nisaaniŋi n na, o me sariga daaŋu, ka digili u El-Betel, a kpali, nisaaniŋi n na Naawunni daa n nyisi o maŋŋa wusi u jemanso o n daa n kpali o beeri zu zora la.

ref: While they were there, Rebekah's personal servant Deborah died. They buried her under an oak tree and called it “Weeping Oak.” God Blesses Jacob at Bethel
text: Niŋi n na, Rabeeka bipoga Debora kpi, ba pi u tikarigu gbinni, a yi Betel falimini puwa. A zu, ba boona nisaaniŋi Kumsi Tiya.

ref: I will give you the land that I promised Abraham and Isaac, and it will belong to your family forever.
text: Tiŋgbaŋaa m ni daa n naa n ti Ibrahim ni Yisaaka la, m naag u ti i, ka m ni naag u ti e yaŋsi”.

ref: Jacob set up a large rock, so that he would remember what had happened there. Then he poured wine and olive oil on the rock to show that it was dedicated to God,
text: Yakubu naa kugiri sabi seela Naawunni n togsi o saani la ti u, ka kpaa buŋŋuura sariga pa o niŋi, ka lee n kpaa kpaam pa o niŋi.

ref: Jacob and his family had left Bethel and were still a long way from Ephrath, when the time came for Rachel's baby to be born.
text: Yakubu ni o nyaaŋa dima n daa n yi Betel wara Efirata, ka na bi leeli. Niŋi n na, dogim paa Rachel, dogim maa mi daa n ba toom ti u.

ref: She was having a rough time, but the woman who was helping her said, “Don't worry! It's a boy.”
text: O dogim maa n daa ba toom maa zu, pogiso n deegira dogim maa yeli u, “Rachel, da zora dabeem, biduu n na e lee n nye.”

ref: Rachel was at the point of death, and right before dying, she said, “I'll name him Benoni.” But Jacob called him Benjamin.
text: O n digili bii maa yuuri Benoni, o vuusini o baasigu vuusim, ka kpi. Ama bii maa ba digili o yuuri Benjamin.

ref: Rachel was buried beside the road to Ephrath, which is also called Bethlehem.
text: A zu, Rachel daa n kpini yi Efirata soori, ba pi u sogbarimini. .

ref: Jacob set up a tombstone over her grave, and it is still there.
text: Yakubu daa n naa kugiri sabi o kaa zu, a ti yini zinni, kugiri maa na sabi Rachel kaa maa zu.

ref: Jacob, also known as Israel, traveled to the south of Eder Tower, where he set up camp.
text: Niŋi n na, Yakubu lee n maali n nyisigi nisaaniŋi naag o gbana suga wa ti gali Migdal-Eda, a sabi.

ref: During their time there, Jacob's oldest son Reuben slept with Bilhah, who was one of Jacob's other wives. And Jacob found out about it. Jacob's Twelve Sons
text: Jemanso Yakubu n daa n zi nisaaniŋi paaligu maa n na, Rubin kpabi o ba pogibila Biliha, o ba n wumi lala yelli maa, o suuri nyisigi. Yakubu biduusi pii ni bayi yela n ŋa.

ref: Jacob went to his father Isaac at Hebron, also called Mamre or Kiriath-Arba, where Isaac's father Abraham had lived as a foreigner.
text: Niŋi n na, Yakubu pori n gaa o ba Yisaaka saani, a yi Mamiri, a leeli Kiriyaf-Aba seela Ibrahim ni Yisaaka n daa n be.

ref: Jacob lived in the land of Canaan, where his father Isaac had lived,
text: Yakubu daa n zinni Keenan tiŋgbaŋŋu niŋi, seela o ba n daa n zi, a niŋi saana la.

ref: “They're not here anymore,” the man replied. “I overheard them say they were going to Dothan.” Joseph left and found his brothers in Dothan.
text: Daba maa yeli u, “Ba lee n ka kpe, m daa wumi ba n yetira taaba, ni ba gaa Dodan tiŋŋa.” Niŋi n na, Yisifu kari ba bobiri gaa Dodan tiŋŋa maa, a paa ba niŋi.

ref: Jacob loved Joseph more than he did any of his other sons, because Joseph was born when Jacob was very old. Jacob had even given Joseph a fancy coat
text: Yakubu kpabira Yisifu ga, a gaari o biisi maa zaa, a kpali, o kurigirisim jemani n na o dogi u, o se tagite wogili so n veela ga ti u.

ref: which showed that Joseph was his favorite son, and so Joseph's brothers hated him and would not be friendly to him.
text: Jemanso o beya n nye maa ba ba kpabira u gaari ba, ba chisi u, ka bi togsira o saani kpabirisim yatoga.

ref: One day, Joseph told his brothers what he had dreamed, and they hated him even more.
text: Dindaso, Yisifu zaasi zaasim, ka togsi o yela ti o beya maa, ba chisi u ga paasi.

ref: Joseph said, “Let me tell you about my dream.
text: O yeli ba, “Ya wumi m zaasimaa.

ref: We were out in the field, tying up bundles of wheat. Suddenly my bundle stood up, and your bundles gathered around and bowed down to it.”
text: Ti zaa ba ni pu, a leera chi kapaasi, noba maa niŋi, m toori nyisigi n damsi n za, ka ya bini mi ti poli n jili u, ka dobi u tiŋŋa.”

ref: His brothers asked, “Do you really think you are going to be king and rule over us?” Now they hated Joseph more than ever because of what he had said about his dream.
text: O beya maa boosi u, “E wusini maa, e ni niŋi ti naaba, ka nyaasira ti?” Ba chisi u ga paasi, kpali o zaasim la ni seema o n yeli la zu.

ref: Joseph later had another dream, and he told his brothers, “Listen to what else I dreamed. The sun, the moon, and eleven stars bowed down to me.”
text: O lee n maali n zaasi zaasim, ka yeli o beya, “Ya wumi, m lee n zaasini zaasim, ŋala wune, wuluŋu, ŋmeriga, a ni ŋmeribiisi pii ni yinni n ti dobi ma tiŋŋa.”

ref: When he told his father about this dream, his father became angry and said, “What's that supposed to mean? Are your mother and I and your brothers all going to come and bow down to you?”
text: O n poli n wusi o ba seema o n yeli o beya la, o ba tasi u, ka boosi u, “Bo zaasim n na e zaasi lala? Yamonsitiri, m ni e ma ni e beya ŋa zaa ni ti dobi i tiŋŋa?”

ref: Joseph's brothers were jealous of him, but his father kept wondering about the dream. Joseph Is Sold and Taken to Egypt
text: O yela daa n marini o beya la pubeerim, ama o ba wune daa n naani yela maa niŋi o fere niŋi. Yisifu beya koosi u

ref: One day when Joseph's brothers had taken the sheep to a pasture near Shechem,
text: Dindaso Yisifu beya daa gaa Sekem, a wa ti nyaasira ba ba buŋkobiri.

ref: his father Jacob said to him, “I want you to go to your brothers. They are with the sheep near Shechem.” “Yes, sir,” Joseph answered.
text: O ba Yakubu daa n booli u yeli, “E beya ba Sekem, a nyaasira buŋkobiri, m ni tumi i ba saani.” Yisifu sagi, “To.”

ref: His father said, “Go and find out how your brothers and the sheep are doing. Then come back and let me know.” So he sent him from Hebron Valley. Joseph was near Shechem
text: “Gaa n wa ti nyaasi e beya maa alaanfiya, a ni buŋkobiri maa ni ba seem, ka pori n ti yeli ma bunso n ba niŋi.” O ba tumi u, o yi Hebiron nantaŋŋu puu chaŋŋi n we Sekem.

ref: Joseph answered, “I'm looking for my brothers who are watching the sheep. Can you tell me where they are?”
text: Yisifu yeli u, “M beya n ta kpe, jaande, ka e baŋŋi seela ba ni ba buŋkobiri ni be, e wusi ma.”

ref: But before he got there, they saw him coming and made plans to kill him.
text: O daa na bani wogila chena, ka o beya maa nye o n chena ba saani. Ba niŋi naŋgbanluŋko maa, ka o yi ta, ba ku u.

ref: They said to one another, “Look, here comes the hero of those dreams!
text: O n chena maa, ba yeli taaba, “Zaasim zaasira la n chena la.

ref: Reuben heard this and tried to protect Joseph from them. “Let's not kill him,” he said.
text: Rubin n wumi n ŋa, o niŋi aniya, seema o n di n niŋi n moligi biyaa yi o beya ŋa saani. Ka yeli ba, “Ti da ku u.

ref: “Don't murder him or even harm him. Just throw him into a well out here in the desert.” Reuben planned to rescue Joseph later and take him back to his father.
text: Ya da kpaag o zeem. Ti naag u lobi n niŋi bulukuuŋaa yindaana niŋi, moogaa puwa, ka da siisi u.” O daa n yelini n ŋa, lala jemani o ni nye daama moligi u, ka mari u wa ti ti o ba.

ref: When Joseph came to his brothers, they pulled off his fancy coat
text: Yisifu n daa n ti paa o beya maa saani, ba gbaag u, ka pirigi o tagite wogiliso, o n daa n pili maa.

ref: and threw him into a dry well.

ref: As Joseph's brothers sat down to eat, they looked up and saw a caravan of Ishmaelites coming from Gilead. Their camels were loaded with all kinds of spices that they were taking to Egypt.
text: Ŋama nyaaŋa, ba zi tiŋŋa ba di bindira, ka nye Isimeel baligu yawdiriba seesi n yi Giliyadi, a wara Yijipiti. Yawdiriba ŋa nyogimasi daa n zinni tulalesi, dugisi, a ni buŋŋusa baligu baligu.

ref: So Judah said, “What will we gain if we kill our brother and hide his body?
text: Juuda boosi o mabiisi maa, “Bo tono n na ti ya n nye, ka ti ku ti sunzo ŋa, ka naag o yela sogi?

ref: Let's sell him to the Ishmaelites and not harm him. After all, he is our brother.” And the others agreed.
text: Ti koosi u ti Isimeel dimaa, o so ti n ku u, koo, a niŋi u daaŋu. Ti sunzo n na, ka ti niŋi zeem luŋko.” Niŋi n na, o taaba maa wumi seema o n yeli maa.

ref: When the Midianite merchants came by, Joseph's brothers took him out of the well, and for
text: Jemanso Isimeel yawdiriba ŋa n ya n ti gaari, o beya maa darigi u nyisi bulukuuŋu la puwa, a koosi u gbitisi pisiyi ti ba, ba mari u gaari Yijipiti.

ref: When Reuben returned to the well and did not find Joseph there, he tore his clothes in sorrow.

ref: Joseph's brothers killed a goat and dipped Joseph's fancy coat in its blood.

ref: After this, they took the coat to their father and said, “We found this! Look at it carefully and see if it belongs to your son.”
text: ka naag u kuli, a wa ti wusi ba ba, ka boosi u, “Nyaasi, bunso ti ni nye, e bii la tagite n ŋa? Koo ka wune n na?”

ref: Jacob knew it was Joseph's coat and said, “It's my son's coat! Joseph has been torn to pieces and eaten by some wild animal.”
text: O nyaasi u, ka baŋŋi u, ka sagi, “Mm, m bii maa tagite n na, ama moo puu buŋ n gbaag u toog u ŋobi.”

ref: Jacob mourned for Joseph a long time, and to show his sorrow he tore his clothes and wore sackcloth.
text: Niŋi n na, Yakubu darigi o bimpinna kasigi, ka naa sufusaaŋu bimpinna pili, a kumi o bii maa kuuri ga.

ref: All of Jacob's children came to comfort him, but he refused to be comforted. “No,” he said, “I will go to my grave, mourning for my son.” So Jacob kept on grieving.
text: O biduusi ni bipunsi zaa daa n ti maagira o sufu, o zagsi, ka yeli ba, “M ni sufusaaŋu ni kumsi n ni gaa kaa puwa, a zu, o ba kpali o zu kumina.”

ref: Meanwhile, the Midianites had sold Joseph in Egypt to a man named Potiphar, who was the king's official in charge of the palace guard.

ref: The Ishmaelites took Joseph to Egypt and sold him to Potiphar, the king's official in charge of the palace guard.
text: Isimeel baligu dima n daa da Yisifu la, ba mari u gaa Yijipiti paaligu, a koosi u ti dabso ba n boona Potifa. Dabaa daa n niŋini Yijipiti paaligu zaa naaba guuriba la kpeema.

ref: So Joseph lived in the home of Potiphar, his Egyptian owner. Soon Potiphar realized that the Lord was helping Joseph to be successful in whatever he did.

text: Ama, ni si n chaari kpeeŋutiri, o naag o tagite basi, ka chee n yi.”

ref: and threw Joseph in the same prison where the king's prisoners were kept. While Joseph was in prison,
text: Yisifu yidaana naag u wa ti niŋi diyoorigu puwa, seela naaba n yoora o niriba, Yisifu daa bani diyoorigu maa puu gba,

text: ka Duuma daa n ba o saani, ka wusira u o nivumantiri, a ti niso n nyaasira diyooriba maa kpabira o yela.

ref: One night each of the two men had a dream, but their dreams had different meanings.
text: Niriba bayi ŋa, Yijipiti naaba n daa ti ba yo ba dii maa, daa n zaasi zaasima, ka zaasima ayi ŋa mi zaa gbina kuri.

ref: with three branches. As soon as it budded, it blossomed, and its grapes became ripe.

ref: I held the king's cup and squeezed the grapes into it, then I gave the cup to the king.”

ref: Joseph said: This is the meaning of your dream. The three branches stand for three days,

text: A kpali bo, ba daa n gbaa mani kpeeŋtiri yi Hiburu dima paaligu. M ni lee n ta kpe ŋa gbaa, m bi niŋi yaso n daga ba yo ma dii.”

ref: When the chief cook saw that Joseph had given a good meaning to the dream, he told Joseph, “I also had a dream. In it I was carrying three breadbaskets stacked on top of my head.
text: Paanu niŋina la ni nye seema Yisifu ni wusi o todaana la zaasim gbinni meesimtiri, o yeli Yisifu, “M gba zaasini zaasim, a nye m ni zi paanusi peeri ata.

ref: The top basket was full of all kinds of baked things for the king, but birds were eating them.”
text: Peso n ba saa la, naaba maa bindira seera baligu baligu ba n naa paanu zum maali, a tomini m zu, ka niinsi ti dira a.”

ref: Joseph said: This is the meaning of your dream. The three baskets are three days,
text: Niŋi n na, Yisifu yeli u, “E zaasim maa gbinni n ŋa, peeri ata la niŋini dabaata.

ref: and in three days the king will cut off your head. He will hang your body on a pole, and birds will come and peck at it.
text: Dabaata sunsuuniŋi, naaba maa ni ti ba nyisi i yi kpe, a wa ti ti ba ŋma e zu, ka le i yagili tii zu, niinsi, a ti dira i.”

ref: Three days later, while the king was celebrating his birthday with a dinner for his officials, he sent for his personal servant and the chief cook.
text: Dabaata nyaaŋa naaba maa dogim daa n na, o laŋsi o tumtuminiba, ba ti di ba noori. Niŋi n na, o nyisi o kobee matira kpeema, a ni o paanu niŋina kpeema la, a yi diyoorigu puu ta tumtuminiba maa zaa yiya.

ref: and had the cook put to death. Everything happened just as Joseph had said it would,

ref: but the king's personal servant completely forgot about Joseph.
